active_paradigms.idea: Replace Gompertz with a generalized (Richards) sigmoid whose asymmetry exponent is a fitted constant, keeping the same monomer/seed power-law rate and lag structure
active_paradigms.description: Use a Richards/generalized-logistic growth curve that interpolates between logistic and Gompertz shapes via a single shape constant, so constant-fitting can choose the right asymmetry per dataset instead of being locked to Gompertz. Build sympy expression: let rate = c0*x1**c1, lag = c2*x1**c3/(1 + c6*x2), z = rate*(x0 - lag), and growth = c4*(1 + c7*sp.exp(-z))**(-1/sp.Abs... ) — but avoid Abs; instead use growth = c4 / (1 + c5*sp.exp(-z))**c6 with c6>0 a smooth asymmetry exponent and a separate baseline c7. To keep numerics safe wrap the inner base as (1 + sp.exp(-z)) which is always >=1 so the power is well defined for any real c6 (no negative base, no log of negatives). Use 8 constants total: c0 rate scale, c1 monomer rate exponent, c2 lag scale, c3 monomer lag exponent, c4 plateau, c5 baseline, c6 asymmetry exponent, c7 seed-lag coupling. Set initial_values so c6=1 (logistic) as a stable start. This single template subsumes the current Gompertz as a limiting case, giving strictly more shape flexibility for held-out concentration curves while only adding one constant.
active_paradigms.what_to_optimize: Reduce per-curve shape mismatch (mean_nmse_val) on datasets where the true curve asymmetry differs from pure Gompertz, especially the heavily-weighted 11-curve and 10-curve evaluation-only datasets.
active_paradigms.cautions: Keep the power base as (1 + exp(-z)) which is strictly >=1 so the variable exponent c6 never hits a negative or zero base; do not use Abs/sign. Bound runtime by keeping z linear in t. Watch that adding c7 does not destabilize unseeded (x2=0) curves — the 1/(1+c6*x2)-style seed factor must reduce cleanly when x2=0. Provide initial_values that start near the known-good Gompertz/logistic regime to keep least_squares stable from one start.

active_paradigms.idea: Use log1p/exp combination to model an autocatalytic secondary-nucleation closed-form: a saturating self-amplifying term where the seed concentration x2 sets the initial amplification offset
active_paradigms.description: Structurally move from a phenomenological sigmoid to the mechanistic closed form for secondary-nucleation-dominated amyloid growth from the Knowles/Meisl framework, whose hallmark is y ~ 1 - (1 + B*exp(kappa*t))^{-1} type self-consistent solutions. Implement a compact, stable version: define amp = c0*x1**c1 (effective elongation/secondary rate from monomer), let drive = amp*x0, and let seed_offset = sp.log1p(c2*x2) representing how seeded curves start further along the amplification (less lag). Then growth = c3*sp.tanh(c4*(drive + seed_offset - c5)) shifted/scaled, or more faithfully growth = c3*(1 - 2/(1 + sp.exp(2*(drive + seed_offset - c5)))) which is tanh rewritten for numerical clarity. Final expression = c3*growth_normalized + c6 baseline. The log1p safely handles x2>=0 (argument >=1, no negative log) and converts the multiplicative seed effect into an additive lag shift exactly as the mechanistic theory predicts. tanh is globally bounded and smooth, so least_squares is well conditioned. Use 7 constants matching current count, so no parsimony penalty change.
active_paradigms.what_to_optimize: Improve generalization across held-out seed concentrations (M0) by encoding the mechanistically-correct additive-lag effect of seeding via log1p, targeting the seeded evaluation-only datasets (CsgA 6-curve, htt Q30 5-curve) where seed scaling matters.
active_paradigms.cautions: tanh saturates symmetrically; if real curves are strongly asymmetric this may underfit the tail — mitigate by allowing the time argument scale c4 and offset c5 to absorb shape. Ensure log1p argument 1+c2*x2 stays positive: constrain via initial c2>=0 start and rely on the smooth penalty of large residuals; since x2>=0, keep c2 initialized small positive. Verify tanh-based form still spans 0->1 after baseline c6 and amplitude c3 fitting; pick initial_values giving a mid-range sigmoid.

active_paradigms.idea: Decouple rate and plateau concentration dependence: make the saturation plateau itself depend on monomer m0 (mass-conservation) rather than treating it as a free constant, using a smooth logistic core
active_paradigms.description: Hypothesis: when whole curves at different m0 are held out, a fixed plateau constant cannot reflect that final aggregate mass scales with available monomer, hurting cross-concentration generalization even though within-curve fits look fine. Keep a stable logistic core but let the plateau carry concentration structure. Build: rate = c0*x1**c1, lag = c2/(1 + c3*x2), core = 1/(1 + sp.exp(-rate*(x0 - lag))) (numerically safe, bounded sigmoid), plateau = c4*x1**c5/(1 + c4*x1**c5) style saturating mass term OR simpler plateau = c4*(1 - sp.exp(-c5*x1)) which smoothly saturates with monomer (always in [0,c4], no singularity). Final expression = plateau*core + c6 baseline. This uses the monomer twice — once for kinetics (rate) and once for final yield (plateau) — matching mass-conservation physics. 7 constants, same complexity bracket. Because the data is globally min-max normalized per dataset, the plateau scaling is identifiable across curves with different m0 sharing one template.
active_paradigms.what_to_optimize: Cross-curve concentration generalization (held-out validation curves at unseen m0), specifically reducing systematic plateau-height errors that inflate NMSE on multi-curve datasets like Ab40 (11 curves) and Ab42 (10 curves).
active_paradigms.cautions: 1 - exp(-c5*x1) requires c5*x1 not hugely negative to avoid overflow; since x1=m0>=0 and starting c5 small positive keeps the exponent bounded — use initial_values with c5 around 0.1. Avoid the plateau term collapsing to zero (set c4 initial ~1). The sigmoid core exponent rate*(x0-lag) is bounded for x0 in [0,1] and finite rate so no overflow. Make sure the lag term 1/(1+c3*x2) reduces to a constant when x2=0 (unseeded), preserving behavior on single-curve datasets.
